level_signals: Owns end-to-end launch execution and feature strategy
Expected to build and deploy applications independently
Expected to run high-stakes demos and BD work
Flat org; titles matter less than quality of what ships

must_haves.raw_text_from_jd: You use AI coding tools daily—not as a novelty, but as part of how you work. You use them to read code, prototype, and move faster.
You have been an engineer or have deep experience with developer tools. You can navigate a codebase without pulling engineers off their work.
You have worked on infrastructure, cloud, or platform products. We are less interested in a narrow consumer product background.
You are an executor. You default to shipping the high-leverage thing now, not spending months exploring what to build.
You can build and deploy an application yourself and reason through technical architecture with engineers.
You move fast and communicate clearly. You are comfortable owning decisions that directly affect the product millions of developers use.

nice_to_haves.raw_text_from_jd: For enterprise-focused work: you have shipped enterprise developer products, you are comfortable with sales, and you are polished but gritty.

scope.surface: Cursor AI coding editor product suite for professional developers and enterprise teams
scope.business_model: B2B and B2C developer tooling, likely SaaS
primary_metrics_inferred: Feature adoption and daily usage by developers
Launch success measured via feedback loops and data
Developer trust and reduction of product friction
core_responsibilities: Building the first version of a feature yourself—prototyping with AI coding tools, getting it in front of users, and iterating until it's ready to hand off or scale with engineering.
Defining feature strategy for capabilities that need to work at scale, across individual developers and enterprise teams.
Owning launch execution end-to-end with PMM: GTM checklists, rollout sequencing, feedback loops, and measuring what worked.
Partnering directly with customers, sales, field engineering, and TAMs to align what we build with what they need.
Prioritizing the 1,000 paper cuts—triaging, sequencing, and making sure the small things that erode trust actually get fixed.
Working with data science on instrumentation, metrics, and measuring success.
Writing changelogs, shaping marketing, and telling the story of what we built clearly enough that developers understand it on first read.

green_flags: Clear, specific description of what PMs actually do day-to-day
Explicitly anti-roadmap-committee — high autonomy and ownership
Flat org structure signals lean bureaucracy
Fast-growing AI-native product with millions of developer users
Onsite process includes a real working project, signaling high-quality hiring bar
red_flags: Strictly in-person (SF or NY only); no remote option stated
No visa sponsorship or work authorization information provided
Very high bar: must be able to independently build and deploy apps — uncommon for a PM role
Role is extremely ambiguous by design; may not suit candidates who prefer structured PM environments
Years of experience not stated, making seniority level difficult to assess externally

fields_inferred_not_stated: seniority.level (inferred from scope of ownership, technical requirements, and org context; no explicit level stated)
scope.business_model (inferred from product description and enterprise/individual developer context)
scope.primary_metrics_inferred (inferred from responsibilities; not explicitly listed in JD)
scope.team_size_estimate (inferred from 'small and talent dense' and 'very flat' org language)
ambiguities: The enterprise-focused bullet ('For enterprise-focused work: you have shipped enterprise developer products...') is conditionally framed, suggesting the role may have sub-tracks or that enterprise experience is preferred but not universally required. Treated as nice-to-have.
No explicit years-of-experience requirement stated anywhere in the JD; seniority level is inferred from responsibilities.
Work authorization and visa sponsorship are entirely unaddressed in the JD.
